title: Marbling on fabric using a carrageenan size
subtitle: Transferring combed and stylus patterns to cotton or silk cloth
excerpt: Fabric marbling uses the same carrageenan size as paper marbling but replaces the alum mordant with an alum fabric-mordant solution. Lightweight cotton or silk takes the pattern most cleanly. The finished piece is heat-set and then hand-washed; the pattern is lightfast but will soften with repeated washing.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894), public domain, chapter on marbling on silk; and from general textile-marbling practice in Arts and Crafts era sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: Treating fabric with an alum solution before marbling so the acrylic pigment bonds to the fibre. Without mordanting, the pattern washes off completely.

content.text: Paper marbling uses a 15% alum water solution to mordant the paper surface.

works similarly but requires a higher alum concentration and a soaking rather than brushing method, because fabric has a greater surface area per square centimetre than paper. The basic process is: soak, dry, marble, heat-set, rinse gently.

attrs.heading: Safety: marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Alum solution at higher concentrations irritates skin and eyes. Wear rubber gloves during fabric mordanting. The carrageenan and acrylic pigments are non-toxic at marbling concentrations. Wash hands after each step.

items.name: Lightweight cotton or silk, pre-washed, prewashed without fabric conditioner
items.description: Muslin, cotton lawn, or habutai silk. Heavy fabric does not transfer well. Pre-wash to remove sizing; do not use fabric conditioner (it repels the mordant).

items.name: Alum solution for fabric: 30 g alum per litre of warm water (slug: alum-potash)
items.description: Double the paper concentration. Mix thoroughly; add the fabric and let it soak for at least 1 hour, ideally overnight.

items.name: Carrageenan size (slug: carrageenan)
items.description: Prepared the day before. Use a tray at least as large as the fabric piece.

content.text: Submerge the fabric in the alum solution and leave for 1 hour minimum. Wring the fabric gently: do not rinse: and hang to dry completely. The fabric should feel slightly stiff when dry. Work with the fabric within 24 hours of mordanting; the alum effectiveness declines after that.

Prepare a pattern on the size as for paper: colour drops, then stylus or comb work. Lay the fabric face-down on the size in the same way as paper: hold it at a bow, release from the centre. Fabric is slightly heavier than paper and may take 45-60 seconds rather than 30. Lift by one corner and hold above the tray to let the excess size drip off.

Rinse the fabric briefly in cold water to remove the surface carrageenan. Pat flat on a clean board and let air dry. Once dry, heat-set by pressing with a medium-hot iron (cotton setting for cotton; silk setting for silk) with a pressing cloth between the iron and the fabric. The heat fuses the acrylic pigment to the fabric fibres. After heat-setting, the fabric can be rinsed gently by hand in cool water; the pattern will hold but lighten slightly.
